chunk_summary: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is prevalent in children with obesity, impacting metabolic health, behavior, and quality of life. Screening requires structured history-taking and physical examination, with polysomnography as the diagnostic standard. Management includes adenotonsillectomy, weight control, and CPAP for persistent cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the relationship between obesity and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in children.
Learn structured screening methods for OSA, including sleep history and physical examination.
Recognize the role of polysomnography and adenotonsillectomy in OSA diagnosis and management.
Identify the importance of continued metabolic follow-up post-OSA treatment in obesity clinics.
key_facts: OSA is common in children with obesity and worsens metabolic risk, behavior, and quality of life.
AAP guidelines recommend in-laboratory polysomnography as the standard diagnostic test for suspected OSA.
Adenotonsillectomy is first-line therapy for OSA in children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy.
Obesity increases OSA risk even without significant tonsillar hypertrophy.
OSA treatment improves cardiometabolic outcomes, especially in children with comorbidities like hypertension or insulin resistance.
India-specific pitfall: metabolic follow-up often stops after ENT surgery, which should be avoided.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: AAP childhood OSAS guideline recommends that children who frequently snore be evaluated for OSAS and that in-laboratory polysomnography is the standard diagnostic test when OSAS is suspected.
document_key_topics: Obstructive sleep apnea in children with obesity
Structured screening and diagnostic pathways for OSA
Management strategies including adenotonsillectomy and CPAP
Importance of continued metabolic follow-up post-OSA treatment
